structured_data.section: Thinking of working in Canada? Understanding what an LMIA (Labour Market Impact Assessment) is can be your first step toward securing a Canadian work permit. This guide explains everything you need to know about LMIA requirements, process, and eligibility

title: LMIA: Labour Market Impact Assessment
content:
A Labour Market Impact Assessment (LMIA) is a document issued by Employment and Social Development Canada (ESDC) that an employer in Canada may need before hiring a foreign worker. According to Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship Canada (IRCC), an LMIA confirms that there is a need for a foreign worker to fill the job and that no Canadian citizen or permanent resident is available to do so.
To obtain a positive LMIA, employers are typically required to advertise the job for at least four weeks and demonstrate genuine efforts to hire Canadian citizens or permanent residents. This includes interviewing qualified applicants and documenting reasons for not hiring them.
ï»¿
LMIA applications are detailed and require lots of documentation and statistical tabulation. Examples include a numerical breakdown of the number of Canadian applicants for the position, the number of offers of employment made, and the number of unqualified applicants. Employers must provide a written description of why each un-hired Canadian was not qualified for the job. Not all work permit types require a LMIA to be issued; work permit streams that are LMIA-exempt come under the International Mobility Program.
_**_Note_** : This page refers to the LMIA process under the Temporary Foreign Worker Program (TFWP). To learn how the LMIA process works in conjunction with the Express Entry immigration selection system, please see our Express Entry page._
Please note that Quebec has some distinct processes and policies regarding LMIAs. For full information about Quebecâ€™s LMIA process, refer .

structured_data.section: What is a Labour Market Impact Assessment (LMIA)?

title: LMIA: Labour Market Impact Assessment
content:
An LMIA is typically required when a Canadian employer wants to hire a foreign worker through the Temporary Foreign Worker Program (TFWP). This process ensures that no qualified Canadian citizen or permanent resident is available to fill the position. Employers must follow specific recruitment efforts, such as advertising the job and assessing local candidates, before being allowed to submit an LMIA application. A positive LMIA confirms that hiring a foreign worker will not negatively affect the Canadian labor market and allows the worker to apply for a work permit.
However, not all foreign workers need an LMIA to work in Canada. Under the International Mobility Program (IMP), employers can hire foreign nationals without going through the LMIA process if the job offers bring broader social, cultural, or economic benefits to Canada. This includes positions covered by international agreements like CUSMA, transfers within multinational companies, post-graduation work permit holders, participants in youth exchange programs, and spouses of certain skilled workers or international students. While an LMIA isnâ€™t required for these roles, employers must still submit the offer through the Employer Portal and pay a compliance fee.

title: LMIA: Labour Market Impact Assessment
content:
Employment and Social Development Canada (ESDC) oversees Canadaâ€™s LMIA process. In their analysis, ESDC will consider the following elements of the job offer:
 * Is the salary offered to the foreign worker consistent with the average for the occupation in the area the position is located?
 * Are the working conditions consistent with labour laws and/or collective bargaining agreements?
 * Is there a labour shortage for that occupation in the area the position is located?
 * Is there an ongoing labour dispute in the company and/or industry?
 * Has the Canadian employer undertaken recruitment efforts in order to find a Canadian to fill the position?
 * Will the foreign worker be able to transfer unique skills or expertise to Canadians?
 * Will hiring the foreign worker help to create or retain jobs for Canadians?
 * Will the foreign worker be the employee of the Canadian employer, whereby the foreign worker is expected to work on a full-time basis at a pre-determined wage?

The TFWP is divided into several streams including:

structured_data.section: LMIA and The Temporary Foreign Worker Program

title: LMIA: Labour Market Impact Assessment
content:
Positions where the wage being offered to the TFW is at or above the provincial/territorial wage threshold (median wage + 20%) will be considered high-wage and follow the high-wage program requirements. Those positions where the wage being offered is below the provincial/territorial wage threshold (median wage + 20%) will be considered low-wage and follow the low-wage program requirements.
The table below depicts prevailing wage threshold, and the 20% hike over median wages is already factored in.

title: LMIA: Labour Market Impact Assessment
content:
| Province/territory | For LMIAs received before June 27, 2025 | For LMIAs received as of June 27, 2025 | 
| --- | --- | --- | 
| Alberta | $35.40 | $36.00 | 
| British Columbia | $34.62 | $36.60 | 
| Manitoba | $30.00 | $30.16 | 
| New Brunswick | $28.85 | $30.00 | 
| Newfoundland and Labrador | $31.20 | $32.40 | 
| Northwest Territories | $47.09 | $48.00 | 
| Nova Scotia | $28.80 | $30.00 | 
| Nunavut | $42.00 | $42.00 | 
| Ontario | $34.07 | $36.00 | 
| Prince Edward Island | $28.80 | $30.00 | 
| Quebec | $32.96 | $34.62 | 
| Saskatchewan | $32.40 | $33.60 | 
| Yukon | $43.20 | $44.40 |

title: LMIA: Labour Market Impact Assessment
content:
**Wage:**
Under the high-wage stream, the wage offered must meet or exceed the highest of either:
 * the median wage for the same occupation and location as listed on Job Bank OR
 * the wage that is within the wage range that the company is paying current employees hired for the same job and work location, and with the same skills and years of experience as the job being offered.

**Advertising:**
1) National Job Bank
2) Two other sources of advertising, one of which must be national in scope.
3) Advertisements must have appeared for at least 28 days consecutively within the 3 months immediately before the LMIA application (at least one should still be active when LMIA decision is made).

title: LMIA: Labour Market Impact Assessment
content:
**Wage:**
Under the low-wage stream, the wage offered must meet or exceed the highest of either:
 * the median wage for the same occupation and location as listed on Job Bank OR
 * the wage that is within the wage range that the company is paying current employees hired for the same job and work location, and with the same skills and years of experience as the job being offered.

**Advertising:**
1) National Job Bank
2) Targeted recruitment efforts for youth.
3) Two other sources of advertising, both of which must target a different underrepresented group.
4) Advertising must be done for at least 8 weeks in the last 3 months (at least one ad must remain posted until LMIA is issued).

title: LMIA: Labour Market Impact Assessment
content:
The GTS exists to fast-track option for certain skilled workers in specific occupations. It aims to help Canadian employers fill skill shortages by hiring highly skilled foreign workers. There are 2 ways in which a position may be eligible for a GTS LMIA, the benefits of which include faster processing times.
 1. Category A- Under Category A of the GTS stream, companies are eligible for a GTS LMIA if they have been referred to the GTS by a designated referral partner. 
 2. Category B- Companies can also qualify under Category B of the GTS stream if the position they are seeking to fill appears on the Global Talent Occupations list.

**Wage:**
To be successful under a GTS application, the company must pay the prevailing wage, which is defined as the highest of either:
 * The regional median hourly wage posted on Job Bank OR
 * the wage that is within the wage range that youâ€™re paying your current employees hired for the same job and work location, and with the same skills and years of experience

In addition to this;
 * Under Category A, for the first 2 unique and specialized positions requested by an employer and approved by the TFWP per calendar year, the salary must be at least $38.46 per hour that amounts to no less than a base salary of $80,000 annually. For any additional unique and specialized positions requested by the employer (in other words, more than 2) and approved by the TFWP per calendar under Category A, at least $72.11 per hour which amounts to no less than a base salary of $150,000 annually
 * Under Category B, for occupations with wage floors, employers must offer the hourly wage rate at minimum.

**Advertising:**
There is no minimum requirement for the GTS.

title: LMIA: Labour Market Impact Assessment
content:
These LMIAs are specific to the agriculture sector. The Agricultural stream is for employers in specific commodity sectors (such as dairy, poultry, maple syrup, and apiary products). To qualify, employers must be hiring for on-farm primary agriculture roles. LMIAs may be issued for 2 or 3 years, depending on whether it is a low-wage or high-wage position. Proof of recruitment advertising is required for the Agricultural stream.
The Seasonal Agricultural Worker Program (SAWP) is designed to help agricultural employers hire temporary foreign workers to address seasonal labour shortages. It has its own set of requirements and processes, including a maximum employment duration of 8 months and restrictions on participating countries.

title: LMIA: Labour Market Impact Assessment
content:
This LMIA allows families to hire foreign caregivers on a live-in or live out basis in 2 categories:
 1. Caregivers for Children under NOC 44100
 2. Caregivers for people with high medical needs under NOC 31301, 32101 or 44101.

**Wage:**
In pursuing an LMIA for In-Home Caregiver positions, employers must meet the program requirements for the Streams for high-wage or Low-wage positions including paying the prevailing wage for the occupation in the location where the work will be performed, and conducting the necessary recruitment and advertisement requirements for the stream.

title: LMIA: Labour Market Impact Assessment
content:
Employers in the province of Quebec may fill selected positions without having to include proof of recruitment efforts. The list of eligible occupations for this facilitated LMIA process is available here (page is in French only).
The occupation list for 2026 was implemented on Feb 24, 2026, however, employers have a transitional period until March 26, 2026, during which they can apply for their LMIA applications under the 2025 list.
In the province of British Columbia, employers must also register with the provincial government in order to hire a temporary foreign worker using an LMIA.

title: LMIA: Labour Market Impact Assessment
content:
For every job position being requested, employers have to pay a CAD $1,000 application fee, unless exempt. This is not per application, but per position.
Therefore, if the LMIA application is requesting 10 positions, the application fees due would be CAD $10,000.

title: LMIA: Labour Market Impact Assessment
content:
For low-wage positions, the duration of work permits set out in LMIAs is limited to a maximum of one year.
For high-wage positions, the duration of work permits set out in LMIAs is limited to a maximum of three years. However, longer LMIAs can be issued in exceptional circumstances where the employer provides a clear reason for the request.

title: LMIA: Labour Market Impact Assessment
content:
Employers using the LMIA process to hire foreign workers under the high-wage stream must show adequate steps to reduce their reliance on temporary foreign workers over time. Employers are expected to proactively engage in activities to recruit, hire, and train Canadians.
Canada requires employers to undertake activities in addition to the existing recruitment and advertising requirements. These include:
1) Three additional recruitment activities.
2) One additional activity targeting under-represented groups (new immigrants, aboriginal people, youth, Canadians with disabilities).
3) One activity to help temporary foreign workers transition to permanent residence (such as providing them with permanent job offers or language training).
Employers are required to prepare projected estimates of the numbers of Canadians/permanent residents they expect to recruit through these activities. They will also need to supply proposed timelines for the activities outlined.
Lastly, employers are required to report back to ESDC with the results of the activities they implemented as part of their transition plan. The results will be compared to their submitted projections and will be evaluated if the employer is audited or seeks to re-apply for a LMIA in the future.
Employers hiring low-wage workers, in-home caregivers, health care providers, Seasonal Agricultural Workers, or supporting an applicantâ€™s permanent residency application are exempt from the transition plan requirement. Employers also do not need a transition plan if the position requires unique skills or is of a limited duration and will no longer exist after the TFW leaves.

title: LMIA: Labour Market Impact Assessment
content:
Employers with 10 or more employees applying for a new LMIA under this stream are subject to a cap. If an employer is hiring for a sector with a demonstrated labour shortage, such as construction, nursing, and food manufacturing, they may hire up to 20 percent of their employees through this stream. Otherwise, employers may hire up to 10 percent of their employees through this stream.
From April 1, 2026, to March 31, 2027, there is also a temporary measure in place to raise the cap on low-wage TFWs hired by rural employers to 15%. This higher cap only applies in participating provinces and territories, outside Census Metropolitan Areas (CMAs).

structured_data.section: Cap on low-wage Temporary Foreign Workers

title: LMIA: Labour Market Impact Assessment
content:
Some low-wage LMIA applications are refused before processing if the job is located in a census metropolitan area (CMA) where the unemployment rate is 6% or higher at the time the application is submitted. The table below shows the unemployment rates used by Service Canada, based on the application submission date. This table is updated every 3 months. | Census metropolitan area | Unemployment rate (%) for applications submitted from October 11, 2024, to January 9, 2025 | Unemployment rate (%) for applications submitted from January 10 to April 3, 2025 | Unemployment rate (%) for applications submitted from April 4 to July 10, 2025 | Unemployment rate (%) for applications submitted from July 11-October 9, 2025 | Unemployment rate (%) for applications submitted from October 10, 2025 to January 8, 2026 | Unemployment rate (%) for applications submitted from January 9, 2026, to April 9, 2026 | Unemployment rate (%) for applications submitted from April 10, 2026, to July 9, 2026 | 
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |

structured_data.section: Refusal to process a LMIA application
Refusal to process a LMIA application
Refusal to process a LMIA application

title: LMIA: Labour Market Impact Assessment
content: Employers are held accountable for the promises they make in their LMIA applications. Particular focus is on the proposed activities in their transition plans. ESDC often follows up with employers through audits. ESDC will also examine submitting results if the employers choose to re-apply.

title: LMIA: Labour Market Impact Assessment
content:
Employers seeking to hire with the support of a LMIA should be able to clearly articulate their business challenges, as well as manage a lot of detail. There is no secret formula to obtaining a positive LMIA.
Itâ€™s all about following detailed guidelines, paying attention to the minutiae, and providing a clearly articulated position. Most Program Officers are well-educated, reasonable people, and understand the wide majority of industries across Canada. They base their decisions on internal labour market indicators, as well as the information submitted by the employer. When these two things correctly intersect, they may issue a positive LMIA.
Program Officers take a hard stance on employers trying to circumvent the system. They donâ€™t take kindly to employers who are being dishonest and fraudulent about their recruitment efforts.
Successful LMIA applications are detailed, logical, and well-articulated pieces of business communication; they are not just a stack of application forms and a few job postings.
It is the duty of the employer, or their representative, to explain clearly to the presiding officer:
1) What means they have taken to recruit or train Canadians.
2) Why they have had difficulty recruiting Canadians.
3) What their plan is to train Canadians so as to not become reliant upon the use of temporary foreign workers.
4) The ways in which the Canadian labour market would benefit from the hiring of temporary foreign workers through the filling of labour shortages, transfer of skills, job creation, etc.
5) The intention of the employer to abide by all applicable Canadian labour laws as they relate to hiring foreign workers.
Obtaining a positive LMIA takes a keen attention to detail and an extensive amount of work. By ensuring that guidelines are followed carefully and making a clear position to the Program Officer, the chance of receiving a favourable result will be greatly increased.

title: LMIA: Labour Market Impact Assessment
content:
Employers may be reluctant to undergo the LMIA process for applicants whose situations are marginal. These situations may include:
 * Working Holiday work permit holders seeking to remain on staff with their current employer at the end of their work permit.
 * Post-graduation work permit holders seeking to remain on staff with their current employer at the end of their work permit.
 * Open work permit holders seeking to remain on staff with their current employer at the end of their work permit.

Many job seekers â€“ particularly those already working in Canada hoping to transition from one work permit to another with an LMIA â€“ have been frustrated by their employerâ€™s lack of knowledge of Canadaâ€™s LMIA process or their employerâ€™s reluctance to engage with the LMIA process to the letter. For example, employers may be reluctant to post a job ad that includes details of the compensation, or may identify the wrong NOC code, or may identify the correct NOC code but fail to list appropriate duties in the job ad. Any of these errors could lead to a failure to obtain the LMIA or needing to start all over again, to the frustration and cost of the employer and the worker.
As the worker, it may be in your best interests to take an active interest in how the LMIA process is being undertaken at your current or potential place of employment. In particular, small- to medium-sized businesses that may be undertaking the LMIA process on your behalf for the first time ever, or the first time in many years, may inadvertently make costly errors due to not being acquainted with the intricacies of the LMIA process. In such a situation, it may be in everyoneâ€™s best interests for a regulated Canadian immigration consultant to oversee the LMIA preparation and application and liaise with the government on the employerâ€™s behalf. We have a short list of recommended consultants here, each of whom has extensive expertise in securing LMIAs for appropriate cases and work permits for foreign workers.

structured_data.section: What else should job seekers know about Canadaâ€™s LMIA process?

title: LMIA: Labour Market Impact Assessment
content:
LMIA processing times can be somewhat unpredictable, and the LMIA process can range from a couple of weeks to a few months. Employers and workers must also take into the four weeks required to advertise the position.
Some LMIA cases are fast-tracked (see above: â€™10-day Processing for Highest-Demand, Highest-Paid and Shortest-Duration Occupationsâ€™).
The government of Canada does not have a publicly stated processing standard or target, and LMIA processing times are not otherwise published online by any government department.

structured_data.section: How long does it take to get a LMIA?

title: LMIA: Labour Market Impact Assessment
content:
Foreign workers wishing to obtain an LMIA must first have an eligible job offer from an employer in Canada. See our employment section for information and resources on finding work in Canada.
Not sure where to find an employer willing to go through the LMIA process? Our database lists Canadian companies that have previously received positive LMIA approvals, searchable by occupation, NOC code, and province. 
Search LMIA-approved employers in Canada â†’

structured_data.section: How can I get an LMIA?

title: LMIA: Labour Market Impact Assessment
content: Itâ€™s quite possible that you can bypass the LMIA process by getting a work permit under one the International Mobility Program streams. For workers and employers alike, it is preferable to get a work permit under the International Mobility Program, as these work permit do not require the worker to obtain a LMIA.

title: LMIA: Labour Market Impact Assessment
content:
For the latest LMIA news and requirements, start with the official Government of Canada resources from IRCC and ESDC, which provide the most accurate information on eligibility, processing, and policy changes. You can also join Moving2Canadaâ€™s community to receive the latest LMIA resources and immigration news for practical explanations, updates, and guidance that help you understand what the changes mean for workers and employers.
Always verify major updates against official government sources before making immigration decisions.
